source: Security Enhancements - Introduction to PAM and basic usage: Where PAM policy and module config live, and how a policy line is written

questions.q: What replaced the traditional single /etc/pam.conf on most distributions?
questions.why: Modern PAM centralizes per-service policy files in /etc/pam.d/.

questions.q: In /etc/pam.d/, how is a service's policy file identified?
options: By its filename, which is the service name
By a header line inside it
questions.why: The service name is the file name, so the same file can serve multiple names via symlink.

questions.q: A line in an /etc/pam.d/ file has which four fields?
options: service, facility, flag, module (five including args)
questions.why: The service is the filename, so the line itself is type/control/module/args.

questions.q: Where do the PAM module .so files live on a 64-bit system?
questions.why: Modules are under /lib64/security/; the path in a line is relative to it.

options: Global per-module configuration files (e.g. limits.conf, pam_env.conf)
questions.why: /etc/security/ holds module-specific configuration; /etc/pam.d/ holds the policies.

questions.q: What does a leading '-' on a line (e.g. -session) mean?
options: If the module does not exist, it will not affect the result or be logged (handy for optional modules)
questions.why: The - prefix tolerates a missing module silently.

options: A version-declaration comment (a convention, like #!/bin/bash), not a functional directive
questions.why: It just declares the config version by convention.

options: It is the central policy for logging into the system, and many other services reuse it via the include keyword
It is the SSH server config
questions.why: system-auth authenticates system login and is include'd by other services to save reconfiguration.
